spans.content: Tensile deformation behavior analysis of low density Fe–18Mn–10Al–
xC steels
Hua Ding n , Dong Han, Jun Zhang, Zhihui Cai, Zhiqiang Wu, Minghui Cai
School of Materials and Metallurgy, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, PR China
a r t i c l e i n f o
Article history:
Received 19 August 2015
Received in revised form
20 November 2015
Accepted 22 November 2015
Available online 23 November 2015
Keywords:
Fe–Mn–Al–C steels
Strain hardening
Ordered phase
C concentration
Crussard–Jaoul (C–J) analysis
a b s t r a c t
Three low density Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels containing 0.5, 0.8 and 1.2 C (wt%) were utilized to in-
vestigate the differences in microstructures and their influence on mechanical behaviors during plastic
deformation. The 0.5C steel had a duplex ferritic–austenitic structure and the fraction of austenite was
, while the fraction of austenite was
for the 0.8C steel and some ordered phases existed
in both ferrite and austenite. In contrast, a fully austenitic microstructure with some κ carbides was
achieved in the 1.2C steel. Due to the existence of the ordered phases by the addition of C from
to
, the 0.8C steel exhibited a highest tensile strength of
{ \sim } 9 7 9 \mathrm { M P a }
, and a moderate strain hardening
capacity. The 1.2C steel revealed a superior combination of strength and ductility (ultimate tensile
strength of 946.7 MPa and elongation to failure of
, with a much more pronounced strain hardening
than the other two steels. The differential Crussard–Jaoul (C–J) analysis demonstrated a two-stage strain
hardening behavior in both 0.5C and 0.8C steels, while a three-stage one in the 1.2C steel. This difference
in strain hardening behavior was further understood in terms of microstructural analysis at the different
stages of plastic deformation.

1. Introduction
The target of high performance steels is to increase strength
and maintain ductility of steels as to satisfy the needs for weight
savings by reducing the thickness of structural automotive com-
ponents. However, the requirement for the stiffness of structures
cannot be satisfied when the thickness of the structure is thinned
to a certain extent. That is why lowering the density of steels has
become one of the research trends in the development of high
performance steels.
In the previous research, it was revealed that the density of Fe–
Mn–C steels can be reduced to
6 . 5 { - } 7 . 1 \ \mathrm { g } / \mathrm { c m } ^ { 3 }
with an addition of 6.5–
Al [1]. Generally, low density steels can be classified into three
categories: (1) ferrite based steels [2,3]; (2) austenite based steels [4–
6] and (3) duplex or triplex steels [1,7–12]. The physical phenomena
in duplex and triplex low density steels are quite complex due to the
different deformation mechanisms in each phase and the interac-
tions among the major phases and the ordered ones. Frommeyer
et al. [1] investigated the high-strength Fe–(18–28)Mn–(9–12)Al–
(0.7–1.2) C triplex steels exhibiting a strength of 700–1100 MPa and a
total elongation up to
or more. Accordingly, the strain hardening
exponents were determined to investigate the tensile deformation
behaviors of the steels. Later, Sutou et al. [7] demonstrated that an
addition of C to Fe–20Mn–10Al–xC alloys, ranging from 0.25 to
linearly increased the tensile strength, with a maximum of
\sim 1 0 9 2 1 \ \mathrm { M P a }
. However, the tensile ductility of the steels first in-
creased, reached a maximum value
at
C and then
declined again (only
at
C. The influence of cooling
patterns on the working hardening curves of the steels was studied
as well, though the microstructural analysis during deformation was
not provided in this study. Hwang et al. [8] studied a duplex Fe–
20Mn–9Al–0.6C steel exhibiting a tensile strength of
\sim 8 0 0
MPa and
an elongation of
. It was pointed out that the strain hardening
exponent of austenite was higher than that of ferrite. Recently, the
strain hardening behavior of a duplex steel with the similar com-
position, Fe–18.1Mn–9.6Al–0.65C, was investigated and multi-stage
strain hardening characteristics were reported [9]. Another work [10]
demonstrated that the Fe–27Mn–12Al–0.8C steel contained ordered
phases in both phases, B2 and
\mathsf { D O } _ { 3 }
in ferrite and κ carbide in aus-
tenite, in which a relatively high yield strength of 812 MPa and
tensile strength of
9 5 5 \mathrm { M P a }
were achieved, with a moderate elon-
gation of
due to a relatively low initial strain hardening rate.
Meanwhile, our preliminary work [12] indicated that an addition of C
ranging from 0.8 to
to the Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels caused a
dramatic change in microstructures and hence in the final mechan-
ical properties of the steels.
Although some studies have been conducted on the micro-
structure–mechanical properties relationship of such low density

Three low density Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels containing 0.5, 0.8 and 1.2 C (wt%) were utilized to in-
vestigate the differences in microstructures and their influence on mechanical behaviors during plastic
deformation. The 0.5C steel had a duplex ferritic–austenitic structure and the fraction of austenite was
, while the fraction of austenite was
for the 0.8C steel and some ordered phases existed
in both ferrite and austenite. In contrast, a fully austenitic microstructure with some κ carbides was
achieved in the 1.2C steel. Due to the existence of the ordered phases by the addition of C from
to
, the 0.8C steel exhibited a highest tensile strength of
{ \sim } 9 7 9 \mathrm { M P a }
, and a moderate strain hardening
capacity. The 1.2C steel revealed a superior combination of strength and ductility (ultimate tensile
strength of 946.7 MPa and elongation to failure of
, with a much more pronounced strain hardening
than the other two steels. The differential Crussard–Jaoul (C–J) analysis demonstrated a two-stage strain
hardening behavior in both 0.5C and 0.8C steels, while a three-stage one in the 1.2C steel. This difference
in strain hardening behavior was further understood in terms of microstructural analysis at the different
stages of plastic deformation.

1. Introduction
The target of high performance steels is to increase strength
and maintain ductility of steels as to satisfy the needs for weight
savings by reducing the thickness of structural automotive com-
ponents. However, the requirement for the stiffness of structures
cannot be satisfied when the thickness of the structure is thinned
to a certain extent. That is why lowering the density of steels has
become one of the research trends in the development of high
performance steels.
In the previous research, it was revealed that the density of Fe–
Mn–C steels can be reduced to
6 . 5 { - } 7 . 1 \ \mathrm { g } / \mathrm { c m } ^ { 3 }
with an addition of 6.5–
Al [1]. Generally, low density steels can be classified into three
categories: (1) ferrite based steels [2,3]; (2) austenite based steels [4–
6] and (3) duplex or triplex steels [1,7–12]. The physical phenomena
in duplex and triplex low density steels are quite complex due to the
different deformation mechanisms in each phase and the interac-
tions among the major phases and the ordered ones. Frommeyer
et al. [1] investigated the high-strength Fe–(18–28)Mn–(9–12)Al–
(0.7–1.2) C triplex steels exhibiting a strength of 700–1100 MPa and a
total elongation up to
or more. Accordingly, the strain hardening
exponents were determined to investigate the tensile deformation
behaviors of the steels. Later, Sutou et al. [7] demonstrated that an
addition of C to Fe–20Mn–10Al–xC alloys, ranging from 0.25 to
linearly increased the tensile strength, with a maximum of
\sim 1 0 9 2 1 \ \mathrm { M P a }
. However, the tensile ductility of the steels first in-
creased, reached a maximum value
at
C and then
declined again (only
at
C. The influence of cooling
patterns on the working hardening curves of the steels was studied
as well, though the microstructural analysis during deformation was
not provided in this study. Hwang et al. [8] studied a duplex Fe–
20Mn–9Al–0.6C steel exhibiting a tensile strength of
\sim 8 0 0
MPa and
an elongation of
. It was pointed out that the strain hardening
exponent of austenite was higher than that of ferrite. Recently, the
strain hardening behavior of a duplex steel with the similar com-
position, Fe–18.1Mn–9.6Al–0.65C, was investigated and multi-stage
strain hardening characteristics were reported [9]. Another work [10]
demonstrated that the Fe–27Mn–12Al–0.8C steel contained ordered
phases in both phases, B2 and
\mathsf { D O } _ { 3 }
in ferrite and κ carbide in aus-
tenite, in which a relatively high yield strength of 812 MPa and
tensile strength of
9 5 5 \mathrm { M P a }
were achieved, with a moderate elon-
gation of
due to a relatively low initial strain hardening rate.
Meanwhile, our preliminary work [12] indicated that an addition of C
ranging from 0.8 to
to the Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels caused a
dramatic change in microstructures and hence in the final mechan-
ical properties of the steels.
Although some studies have been conducted on the micro-
structure–mechanical properties relationship of such low density
Fe–Mn–Al–C steels, the strain hardening behavior of duplex Fe–
Mn–Al–C alloy system, especially those containing ordered phases
has not been fully understood yet. It is well known that the
Crussard–Jaoul (C–J) analysis (i.e.
\ln ( \mathrm { d } \sigma / \mathrm { d } \varepsilon )
vs. lnε) is considered
as a useful tool to indicate the relative changes in work hardening
behaviors over a range of plastic strains, especially in materials
that exhibit microstructural evolutions (e.g. mechanical twinning
or martensitic transformation) upon loading. Meanwhile, as a
strong austenite stabilizer, an addition of C to high Mn and high Al
alloys can significantly increase the volume fraction of austenite
and also lead to the formation of ordered phases. In the present
work, therefore, a Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC alloy system containing
three different C contents was chosen to investigate the effects of
the microstructures on the mechanical behavior of the steels. A
particular emphasis will be placed on the clarification of the dif-
ferences in mechanical behaviors in the steels consisting of dif-
ferent phase constituents by
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis, which can provide some
useful data for alloy design and applications of low density Fe–
Mn–Al–C steels.
2. Experimental
Three different Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC (0.5, 0.8 and
1 . 2 C , \mathsf { w t \% } .
steels
were selected, and the chemical compositions of the steels are
shown in Table 1. The densities of three steels were calculated [7]
6 . 7 8 ~ \mathrm { g } / \mathrm { c m } ^ { 3 }
, respectively. The stacking fault en-
ergy (SFE) values of austenite in 0.5C and 0.8C steels are 73.4 and
7 7 . 9 \mathrm { m J } / \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }
and that of 1.2C steel is
8 4 . 5 ~ \mathrm { m J } / \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }
according to the
model we used previously [13]. The
5 0 \mathrm { k g }
ingots of steels were
prepared by induction melting under an argon atmosphere. The
ingots were forged at
1 2 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,
, and then hot rolled after homo-
genization at the same temperature for
^ { 3 \mathrm { ~ h ~ } }
to the thickness of
3 . 5 \mathrm { m m }
with a reduction of
. The hot-rolled strips were an-
nealed at
1 0 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }
for
^ { 1 \mathrm { ~ h ~ } }
and then cold rolled to a thickness of
1 \mathrm { m m }
. Then, the cold rolled strips were annealed at
1 0 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathsf C ,
fol-
lowed by water quenching to room temperature.
Tensile tests were carried out at room temperature using a
universal testing machine (SANSCMT5000) operating at a constant
crosshead displacement velocity (equivalent to an initial strain
rate of
. Optical microscopy specimens were pre-
pared by standard mechanical polishing procedures and etching
with a solution of
1 ~ \mathrm { m l }
hydrochloric acid,
1 0 0 ~ \mathrm { { m l } }
methanol and
_ { 4 } \mathrm { g }
picric acid. The volume fractions of different phases were mea-
sured by image analysis technique. Thin foils for transmission
electron microscopy (TEM) observations were prepared by twin-
jet electropolishing by using
perchloric acid in methanol at
- 3 0 ^ { \circ } \mathsf C .
TEM analysis was performed in a Tecnai
TEM at an
acceleration voltage of
2 0 0 \mathrm { k V } ,
3. Results
3.1. Microstructures before tensile deformation
Fig. 1 shows the representative optical microstructures of the
as-quenched steels, revealing that 0.5C and 0.8C steels have a
duplex ferritic–austenitic microstructure, while 1.2C steel is fully
austenitic with some annealing twins in grains. The volume frac-
tions of austenite are determined to be
and
in 0.5C
and 0.8C steels, respectively. Detailed microstructure analysis un-
der TEM reveals that no precipitates exist in both austenite (Fig. 2
(a)) and ferrite (Fig. 2(b)) phases of 0.5C steel. In comparison, the
diffraction patterns (SADPs) reveal some weak extra spots be-
tween the fundamental reflections in 0.8C and 1.2C steels, in-
dicating the existence of precipitation particles, shown in Fig. 2(c),
Table 1
Chemical compositions of the investigated steels (mass, %).
(e) and (f). Further indexing of these SADPs confirms that 0.8C
steel contains a κ carbide phase
[ ( \mathrm { F e } , \mathrm { M n } ) _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l C } _ { x } ]
in austenite (Fig. 2
(c)) [14], and some B2 domains with fine
\mathsf { D O } _ { 3 }
particles in ferrite
(Fig. 2(e))[10]; whereas 1.2C steel contains some κ carbides in the
matrix of austenite (Fig. 2(f)). As both ferrite and austenite phases
contain ordered phases in
0 . 8 \mathsf { A l }
steel, the steel is referred to
complex low density steel in the present work.
3.2. Mechanical properties and flow behavior
The engineering stress–strain curves of the steels are plotted in
Fig. 3. A summary of the tensile properties of the present low
density steels with three different C contents are listed in Table 2.
As is observed with increasing C content from
and
, the
total elongation (TE) is found to increase, reaching a maximum of
at
1 . 2 \% \mathrm { ~ C ~ }
; whereas the ultimate tensile strength (UTS) first
increases, reaching a maximum value of
\mathord { \sim } 9 7 9
MPa at
, and
then drops slightly to
\sim 9 4 7
MPa at
C. 1.2C steel shows a su-
perior combination of strength and ductility, i.e. the
\mathrm { U T S } \times \mathrm { T E }
va-
lue can reach as high as
5 3 . 2 \ : \mathrm { G P a \% }
, which is almost twice as high
as that of 0.5C steel.
The variation of strain hardening rate,
\mathbf { d } \sigma / \mathbf { d } \varepsilon
, with true strain of
the three experimental steels are shown in Fig. 4, indicating quite
different strain hardening behaviors. Here, the
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis has
been conducted to investigate the difference in strain hardening
behavior of the steels. It can be found in Fig. 5 that 0.5C and 0.8C
steels exhibit a two-stage strain hardening, while 1.2C steel
manifests a three-stage strain hardening. Accordingly, in order to
further investigate the influence of the initial microstructures on
their deformation mechanisms, the microstructural changes dur-
ing plastic deformation have been examined using a series of in-
terrupted tensile tests combined with TEM.
3.3. Microstructural evolution during tensile deformation
The microstructural evolution of 0.5C steel during deformation is
shown in Fig. 6. In the early deformation stage (
true strain),
the traces of planar slip can be readily observed in austenite (Fig. 6
(a)) and some dislocation tangles are also visible in ferrite (Fig. 6
(b)). As deformation proceeds to a true strain of 0.18, intensive
dislocation slip appears (Fig. 6(c)), and Taylor lattice (Fig. 6(d)) and
microbands (Fig. 6(e)) have developed in austenite; whereas dense
dislocation tangles can be observed in ferrite (Fig. 6(f)).
Fig. 7 shows the microstructural changes at different deforma-
tion stages of 0.8C steels under tension. At the beginning of de-
formation
{ \varepsilon } \mathrm { = } 0 . 0 1
), traces of planar slip can be found in austenite
(Fig. 7(a)), and some dislocations exhibit a typical paired super-
dislocation feature in ferrite (Fig. 7(b)). At a true strain of 0.18, mi-
crobands have formed (Fig. 7(c)) in austenite, while a distinct in-
crease in the dislocation density appear in ferrite (Fig. 7(d)).
Fig. 8 represents the microstructure changes during tensile de-
formation of 1.2C steel. Similar to 0.5C and 0.8C steels, planar slip
can be found at a true strain of 0.02 (Fig. 8(a)). However, Taylor
lattice-like structure has been easily observed in some regions of
austenite when strained to 0.07 (Fig. 8(b)). Well-developed micro-
bands can be observed at strain of 0.3 (Fig. 8(c)), and the interaction
between microbands occur at a strain as large as 0.4 (Fig. 8(d)).

4. Discussions
There are several factors influencing the mechanical properties
of the duplex, triplex or complex high Mn steels such as alloying
elements, phase fraction, grain size as well as characteristics of
each constituent phase. In Fe–Mn–Al–C system, Mn and C are
austenite stabilizers and Al is a ferrite stabilizer. Meanwhile, var-
ious ordered phases appear in this system due to the heavy

4. Discussions
There are several factors influencing the mechanical properties
of the duplex, triplex or complex high Mn steels such as alloying
elements, phase fraction, grain size as well as characteristics of
each constituent phase. In Fe–Mn–Al–C system, Mn and C are
austenite stabilizers and Al is a ferrite stabilizer. Meanwhile, var-
ious ordered phases appear in this system due to the heavy
alloying of the elements even in the quenched state after solution
treatment [10]. In the present work, the initial microstructures are
quite different in 0.5C and 0.8C steels. Even if the C content in-
creases by

ordered phases must exert effects on the mechanical properties
and deformation behavior of the steels.

4.1. Influence of microstructures on mechanical properties
As is observed with increasing C content from
to
in
Table 2, the yield strength (YS) and UTS of 0.8C steel are much
higher than those of 0.5C steel, which mainly results from the
combined effects of the ordered phases formed in both ferrite and
austenite. It is interesting to notice that the ductility of 0.8C steel is
also higher than that of 0.5C steel, which must be associated with
the higher and more extended strain hardening rate of the steel,
shown in Fig. 4. On the other hand, the YS values of 0.8C and 1.2C
steels are similar, though 0.8C steel has a much smaller grain size
than 1.2C steel. This may be explained by the fact that a larger
fraction of densely populated κ carbides by C addition contributes
to an increase in the yield strength of 1.2C steel. The ductility of
1.2C steel is superior to those of other two steels due to the ex-
tended strain hardening.
In the present work, 0.8C steel possesses a tensile strength of
\mathord { \sim } 9 7 9
MPa and an elongation of
, and the product of
strength and elongation is as high as
3 7 5 9 7 \mathrm { \ : M P a \cdot \% }
, which satisfies
the requirement for high performance automobile steels. Its spe-
cific strength is
1 4 2 . 3 \mathrm { M P a } \mathrm { c m } ^ { 3 } / \mathrm { g }
, which is also quite competitive.
The mechanical properties of the alloy are superior to the result of
881 MPa and
in the
\mathrm { F e { - } } 2 0 \mathrm { M n { - } } 1 0 \mathrm { A l { - } } 1 . 2 \mathrm { C }
steel reported in
Ref. [7]. The strength and elongation of the present studied steel
are also comparable to 995 MPa and
, which has been reported
in the as-quenched Fe–27Mn–12Al–0.8C steel [10]. Therefore, it
can be concluded that the same or a higher level of strength and
ductility can be achieved with a lower Mn content or C content by
controlled thermo-mechanical processes and subsequent heat
treatment schedules.
4.2. Influence of microstructures on strain hardening behavior
As clearly observed in Fig. 4, the microstructural changes have
significant effects on the strain hardening rate vs true strain. The
1.2C steel sustains a sufficient strain hardening rate to prevent the
accumulation of large strains in local regions after 0.026 strain,
while 0.5C and 0.8C steels exhibit the trend of a decreasing strain
hardening rate with true strain curves, though the slopes vary to
some extent upon straining.
Normally, the tensile stress–strain curves were analyzed by the
following several approaches, such as Hollomon analysis, the dif-
ferential Crussard–Jaoul (C–J) analysis and the modified
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
ana-
lysis [15]. So far, strain hardening behavior of dual-phase steels
have been investigated extensively [16–18]. It is known that there
is an apparent difference in elastic and plastic behavior in soft
ferrite and hard martensite, so that strain partitioning between
two phases occurs upon loading. Thus, it was stated that Hollomon
analysis is not applicable to describe the strain hardening behavior
of the ferrite–martenisite dual phase steels [16]. Another research
work [17] also demonstrated that Hollomon analysis can predict
the initial strain hardening rate in a small strain range, but it
cannot reflect the multi-stage strain hardening. Similarly, ferrite
and austenite exhibit distinct strain hardening behaviors due to
their structural differences, and meanwhile the existence of or-
dered phases would exert effects on their deformation behaviors.
As demonstrated in Section 3.3, as deformation proceeds the
complex microstructural changes happen so that a multi-stage
strain hardening is expected. Therefore, the
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis has been
utilized in this study to analyze the strain hardening behaviors of
the investigated steels.
As plotted in Fig. 5, the
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis can describe the strain
hardening behaviors of Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels fairly well. For
example, a two-stage strain hardening is observed for 0.5C steel
(Fig. 5(a)), which agrees with the microstructural changes, as
shown in Fig. 6. In the first stage, planar slip in austenite starts
(Fig. 6(a)), whereas dislocation tangles form in ferrite(Fig. 6(b)). In
the second stage, a low energy Taylor lattice structure, which is
equivalent to the dislocation tangles in materials with wavy slip
and microbands develops in austenite (Fig. 6(d) and (e)), and more
dislocation tangles form in ferrite (Fig. 6(f)), resulting in a rela-
tively high strain hardening.
It was reported that the C–J analysis was less sensitive to the
changes of the ferritic matrix and the shape and distributions of
the second phase martensite which were altered by heat treat-
ment [18], therefore, the modified C–J analysis was utilized

4.2. Influence of microstructures on strain hardening behavior
As clearly observed in Fig. 4, the microstructural changes have
significant effects on the strain hardening rate vs true strain. The
1.2C steel sustains a sufficient strain hardening rate to prevent the
accumulation of large strains in local regions after 0.026 strain,
while 0.5C and 0.8C steels exhibit the trend of a decreasing strain
hardening rate with true strain curves, though the slopes vary to
some extent upon straining.
Normally, the tensile stress–strain curves were analyzed by the
following several approaches, such as Hollomon analysis, the dif-
ferential Crussard–Jaoul (C–J) analysis and the modified
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
ana-
lysis [15]. So far, strain hardening behavior of dual-phase steels
have been investigated extensively [16–18]. It is known that there
is an apparent difference in elastic and plastic behavior in soft
ferrite and hard martensite, so that strain partitioning between
two phases occurs upon loading. Thus, it was stated that Hollomon
analysis is not applicable to describe the strain hardening behavior
of the ferrite–martenisite dual phase steels [16]. Another research
work [17] also demonstrated that Hollomon analysis can predict
the initial strain hardening rate in a small strain range, but it
cannot reflect the multi-stage strain hardening. Similarly, ferrite
and austenite exhibit distinct strain hardening behaviors due to
their structural differences, and meanwhile the existence of or-
dered phases would exert effects on their deformation behaviors.
As demonstrated in Section 3.3, as deformation proceeds the
complex microstructural changes happen so that a multi-stage
strain hardening is expected. Therefore, the
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis has been
utilized in this study to analyze the strain hardening behaviors of
the investigated steels.
As plotted in Fig. 5, the
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis can describe the strain
hardening behaviors of Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels fairly well. For
example, a two-stage strain hardening is observed for 0.5C steel
(Fig. 5(a)), which agrees with the microstructural changes, as
shown in Fig. 6. In the first stage, planar slip in austenite starts
(Fig. 6(a)), whereas dislocation tangles form in ferrite(Fig. 6(b)). In
the second stage, a low energy Taylor lattice structure, which is
equivalent to the dislocation tangles in materials with wavy slip
and microbands develops in austenite (Fig. 6(d) and (e)), and more
dislocation tangles form in ferrite (Fig. 6(f)), resulting in a rela-
tively high strain hardening.
It was reported that the C–J analysis was less sensitive to the
changes of the ferritic matrix and the shape and distributions of
the second phase martensite which were altered by heat treat-
ment [18], therefore, the modified C–J analysis was utilized
instead. The modified
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis revealed the duplex steels de-
form in two stages. The first stage could be associated with the
deformation of the ferritic matrix and the second stage was
probably induced by the uniform strain of ferrite and martensite.
In the present case, the
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis can describe the strain hard-
ening behavior of the duplex Fe–18Mn–10Al–0.5C steel. The rea-
son may be because the features of ferrite and martensite are quite
different and the increase in stress in matensite is dominant in
strain hardening; whereas the difference in hardness between
ferrite and austenite is not quite obvious [8,9] so that the increase
in strain can reflect the strain hardening behavior of the 0.5C steel.

\ln ( d \sigma / d \varepsilon )
vs lnε curve for Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels (a) 0.5C steel, (b) 0.8C steel, and (c) 1.2C steel.
instead. The modified
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis revealed the duplex steels de-
form in two stages. The first stage could be associated with the
deformation of the ferritic matrix and the second stage was
probably induced by the uniform strain of ferrite and martensite.
In the present case, the
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis can describe the strain hard-
ening behavior of the duplex Fe–18Mn–10Al–0.5C steel. The rea-
son may be because the features of ferrite and martensite are quite
different and the increase in stress in matensite is dominant in
strain hardening; whereas the difference in hardness between
ferrite and austenite is not quite obvious [8,9] so that the increase
in strain can reflect the strain hardening behavior of the 0.5C steel.
A comparison of Fig. 5(a) and (b) indicates that the character-
istics of two-stage hardening have not changed with the existence
of the ordered precipitates in 0.8C steel (Fig. 5(b)), even though the
slope in the first stage is slightly steeper in 0.8C steel than that in
0.5C steel. It was clarified that the strain hardening deteriorates
when κ carbides appear in the austenite matrix [19]. Therefore, the
slightly faster decline in strain hardening in 0.8C steel than in 0.5C
steel is most likely to be attributed to the presence of
\boldsymbol { \kappa }
carbides in
the austenitic phase. This has also been evidenced by the occur-
rence of multi-slip in austenite of 0.5C steel (Fig. 6(a)) even at a
low strain, but a dominant single slip in austenite of 0.8C steel
(Fig. 7(a)) as the presence of the ordered phase makes dislocation
multiplication difficult [10].
In the second stage, however, the strain hardening in 0.8C steel
is somewhat stronger and also extends to higher strains than that
in 0.5C steel, shown in Figs. 4 and 5. This seems to be in contra-
diction with the aforementioned effect. In our previous work in
Fe–12Mn–8Al–0.8C steel which consists of austenite, ferrite and κ
carbides, a slowly decreasing rate of strain hardening has been
demonstrated in the second stage of deformation by the modified
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis [11]. The modified
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis has also been con-
ducted for the present 0.8C steel, but no obvious descending has
been found. Fe–18Mn–10Al–0.8C steel (0.8C steel in the present
work) and Fe–12Mn–8Al–0.8C steel consist of similar level of vo-
lume fraction of austenite
and the difference between
these two steels is whether ordered phases exist in ferrite.
Therefore, it can be deduced that the strengthened ferrite phase
resulting from the existence of the ordered phases may affect the
strain partition between ferrite and austenite in the investigated
0.8C steel, causing the increasing trend in strain hardening as
deformation proceeds.
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis can describe the sensitivity of strain hardening of
materials to the changes of internal lattice defects such as dis-
location densities and volume fraction of twins. For fully austenitic
TWIP steels, the
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis has been conducted to investigate the
strain hardening behavior with respect to the microstructure
evolution and the contribution of different deformation mechan-
isms [20,21]. However, the studies on the stain hardening behavior
by using the
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis in high stacking fault energy austenite-
based alloys without TWIP effect have not been reported to our
best knowledge. In the present work, 1.2C steel exhibits a three-
stage strain hardening behavior (Fig. 5(c)), which is different from
0.5C and 0.8C steels.
According to the microstructure analysis during plastic de-
formation of 1.2C steel, the planar slip develops in the primary
deformation stage (Fig. 8(a)), followed by the formation of Taylor
lattice (Fig. 8(b)) and microbands (Fig.8(c)) (intermediate stage)

A comparison of Fig. 5(a) and (b) indicates that the character-
istics of two-stage hardening have not changed with the existence
of the ordered precipitates in 0.8C steel (Fig. 5(b)), even though the
slope in the first stage is slightly steeper in 0.8C steel than that in
0.5C steel. It was clarified that the strain hardening deteriorates
when κ carbides appear in the austenite matrix [19]. Therefore, the
slightly faster decline in strain hardening in 0.8C steel than in 0.5C
steel is most likely to be attributed to the presence of
\boldsymbol { \kappa }
carbides in
the austenitic phase. This has also been evidenced by the occur-
rence of multi-slip in austenite of 0.5C steel (Fig. 6(a)) even at a
low strain, but a dominant single slip in austenite of 0.8C steel
(Fig. 7(a)) as the presence of the ordered phase makes dislocation
multiplication difficult [10].
In the second stage, however, the strain hardening in 0.8C steel
is somewhat stronger and also extends to higher strains than that
in 0.5C steel, shown in Figs. 4 and 5. This seems to be in contra-
diction with the aforementioned effect. In our previous work in
Fe–12Mn–8Al–0.8C steel which consists of austenite, ferrite and κ
carbides, a slowly decreasing rate of strain hardening has been
demonstrated in the second stage of deformation by the modified
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis [11]. The modified
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis has also been con-
ducted for the present 0.8C steel, but no obvious descending has
been found. Fe–18Mn–10Al–0.8C steel (0.8C steel in the present
work) and Fe–12Mn–8Al–0.8C steel consist of similar level of vo-
lume fraction of austenite
and the difference between
these two steels is whether ordered phases exist in ferrite.
Therefore, it can be deduced that the strengthened ferrite phase
resulting from the existence of the ordered phases may affect the
strain partition between ferrite and austenite in the investigated
0.8C steel, causing the increasing trend in strain hardening as
deformation proceeds.
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis can describe the sensitivity of strain hardening of
materials to the changes of internal lattice defects such as dis-
location densities and volume fraction of twins. For fully austenitic
TWIP steels, the
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis has been conducted to investigate the
strain hardening behavior with respect to the microstructure
evolution and the contribution of different deformation mechan-
isms [20,21]. However, the studies on the stain hardening behavior
by using the
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis in high stacking fault energy austenite-
based alloys without TWIP effect have not been reported to our
best knowledge. In the present work, 1.2C steel exhibits a three-
stage strain hardening behavior (Fig. 5(c)), which is different from
0.5C and 0.8C steels.
According to the microstructure analysis during plastic de-
formation of 1.2C steel, the planar slip develops in the primary
deformation stage (Fig. 8(a)), followed by the formation of Taylor
lattice (Fig. 8(b)) and microbands (Fig.8(c)) (intermediate stage)
Fig. 6. Microstructural evolution of 0.5C steel (TEM) (a)–(b)

: (a) planar slip (γ), (b) dislocation tangles (α), (c)–(f)
\varepsilon { = } 0 . 1 8
: (c) intensive dislocation slip (γ), (d) Taylor
lattice (γ), (e) microbands (γ), (f) dense dislocation tangles (α).
and the interaction between microbands in the final stage (Fig. 8
(d)). Relating the microstructural evolution to the
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis, it
can be deduced that the fast decline of strain hardening in the first
stage of 1.2C steel could be mainly related to the easiness of planar
slip in the austenite matrix. In the second stage, strain hardening
can maintain in large strain regions, which is closely associated
with the formation of Taylor lattice and microbands. In the last
stage, a continuous increase in strain hardening could be ex-
plained by a further increase in the density of microbands and the
interaction between microbands. The sustained strain hardening
to higher strains enables
_ { 1 . 2 ~ \mathsf { C } }
steel to possess a much higher
tensile ductility than 0.5C and 0.8C steels. In contrast, the inter-
action between microbands has not appeared under TEM due to
the limited plastic deformation capability in 0.5C and 0.8C steels,
and thus the absence of the 3rd stage leads to a lower tensile
ductility in these two steels. In the present work, the
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis
can be utilized to describe the multi-stage strain hardening be-
havior of the austenite based 1.2C steel, demonstrating that such a
multi-stage hardening characteristic is a result of the variation of
dislocation structures.
From the analysis above, it can be concluded that the
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis can describe the strain hardening behavior of duplex,
complex and austenite based low density Fe–Mn–Al–C steels fairly
well. Meanwhile, the changes of C content in Fe–Mn–Al steels
significantly affect the microstructure-property relationship of Fe–
Mn–Al–C steels and the resulting mechanical behavior can be
controlled by manipulating the alloying elements.
5. Conclusions
The present work investigates the influence of microstructure
differences on strain hardening behaviors during tensile de-
formation of Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC alloys containing 0.5, 0.8 and
1 . 2 \mathsf { C }
( \mathrm { w t \% } )
. The main findings can be summarized as follows.
1. The 0.5C and 0.8C steels exhibited a duplex ferritic–austenitic
structure, while ordered phases (B2 and
\mathsf { D O } _ { 3 }
) and
\boldsymbol { \kappa }
carbides
formed in ferrite and austenite of 0.8C steel, respectively. The
1.2C steel was fully austenitic with some κ carbides.

3. The
{ \mathsf { C } } { \mathsf { - J } }
analysis can be utilized to evaluate the strain hardening
behavior of Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels properly. It was revealed
that 0.5C and 0.8C steels exhibited a two-stage strain hardening,
while 1.2C steel manifested a three-stage strain hardening.
4. The hardening mechanisms at different deformation stages
were further verified by microstructural changes during tensile
testing. The influence of microstructure differences on strain
hardening behaviors was well understood.
